source.title: Preschoolers understand the normativity of cooperatively structured competition

confidence_rationale: Strong empirical design testing a nuanced prediction: children should monitor not just rule compliance but also strategic rationality (trying to win), both of which are normatively required in competitive games. Finding that 5-year-olds but not 3-year-olds grasp the dual-level structure maps to MECH-197's developmental timeline. High source quality (Max Planck group, JECP). Mapping fidelity is high for ARC-049/INV-059 and for the frame complexity increase at games-with-rules stage.
failure_signatures: Three-year-olds only protested rule breaches from irrational opponents, not from rational ones -- full dual-level understanding comes after age 3, consistent with MECH-197 but indicating late timing.
Many 3-year-olds refrained from protesting altogether, suggesting partial understanding only.
Puppet-as-opponent paradigm may not fully capture naturalistic peer game dynamics.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Games-with-rules require children to simultaneously monitor two normative levels: rule-level compliance (no cheating) and strategic rationality (actually trying to win). Five-year-olds but not 3-year-olds fully grasp this dual-level structure and protest violations at both levels.
mapping.ree_translation: This directly evidences that the ARC-049 frame in games-with-rules is STRUCTURED -- not just 'this is play' but 'this is play with these specific constraints AND this rational engagement requirement.' Children at 5 maintain a richer shared-state model than at 3, consistent with MECH-197's progression and the claim that games-with-rules require ongoing shared-state monitoring. The dual-level monitoring (rule + strategy) is necessarily continuous: you must track whether the opponent is trying to win throughout the game, not just at boundaries.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The 'ongoing monitoring' interpretation is inferred -- the paradigm presents discrete events (opponent's moves) and measures discrete responses (protest). Whether children actively monitor between events or sample game state reactively at each move cannot be determined.
mapping.source_context: 3- and 5-year-olds (N=48), competitive game with puppet opponent, Max Planck Institute.

confidence_components.notes: Excellent study design with a nuanced prediction. Strong developmental psychology lab. Mapping fidelity high for frame-structure claims; Q-035 continuous-vs-discrete monitoring cannot be directly resolved.
